metaDescription: Complete Rummy Nose Tetra care guide. Learn about tank size (20+ gal), water parameters, diet, compatible tank mates, and more.

name: Serpae Tetra
diet: Omnivore — flakes, micro pellets, frozen bloodworms, brine shrimp
description: Serpae tetras are vibrant blood-red schooling fish with a distinctive black comma-shaped marking behind the gill plate. While beautiful, they have a reputation as fin nippers — especially in small groups. Keeping them in schools of 8+ in a spacious tank significantly reduces this behavior. They add bold color to community tanks when kept properly.
careGuide:
Tank Setup

  • Tank size: 20 gallons minimum for a school of 8. Their semi-aggressive nature means they need space to establish territories within the school.
  • Plants: Heavily planted tanks with plenty of visual barriers help reduce aggression
  • Substrate: Dark substrate makes their red coloration pop

Water Parameters

  • Temperature: 72–82°F (22–28°C)
  • pH: 5.5–7.5
  • GH: 5–15 dGH

Managing Fin Nipping

Serpae tetras are notorious fin nippers, but this behavior can be managed:

  • Keep 8+ together: In large groups, they establish a pecking order within the school and direct aggression inward rather than at tank mates
  • Avoid long-finned fish: Bettas, angelfish, fancy guppies, and gouramis are targets
  • Provide space: Overcrowded tanks increase aggression
  • Dense planting: Visual barriers break lines of sight and reduce chasing

Tank Mates

Best with other robust, fast-moving fish:

  • Other semi-aggressive tetras (black skirt, Buenos Aires)
  • Barbs (cherry barbs, tiger barbs)
  • Bottom dwellers (corydoras, bristlenose plecos, kuhli loaches)

Absolutely avoid with any slow-moving, long-finned fish.

Frequently Asked Questions

Are serpae tetras aggressive?

They are fin nippers more than truly aggressive. In schools of 8+ with appropriate tank mates, the nipping is usually contained within the school and is rarely harmful.
metaTitle: Serpae Tetra Care Guide: Tank Size, Diet & Tank Mates | World of Aquariums
metaDescription: Complete Serpae Tetra care guide. Learn about tank size (20+ gal), water parameters, diet, compatible tank mates, and more.

name: Black Skirt Tetra
diet: Omnivore — flakes, pellets, frozen bloodworms, brine shrimp, blanched vegetables
description: Black skirt tetras (also sold as black widow tetras) are hardy, easy-to-care-for schooling fish with a distinctive flowing black anal fin that gives them their name. They come in natural silver-and-black and various dyed/modified versions (GloFish). The natural form is the healthiest choice. They can be mild fin nippers in small groups but are generally well-behaved community fish.
careGuide:
Tank Setup

  • Tank size: 15 gallons for a school of 6, 20+ for 8–10
  • Plants: Moderate planting with open swimming areas
  • Flow: Moderate — they enjoy some current

Water Parameters

  • Temperature: 70–82°F — very tolerant of a wide range
  • pH: 6.0–7.5
  • GH: 5–15 dGH

Diet & Feeding

Extremely easy to feed. They accept virtually all foods: flakes, pellets, frozen, freeze-dried, and even blanched vegetables. Feed twice daily, only what they consume in 2–3 minutes.

Tank Mates

Good with most community fish of similar size. Can be mild fin nippers with very long-finned species, so avoid bettas and fancy guppies. Pair well with other tetras, rasboras, barbs, corydoras, and small catfish.

GloFish Variant

The GloFish "Electric Green," "Starfire Red," and other colored versions are genetically modified black skirt tetras with fluorescent genes. Care requirements are identical to the natural form. They are not dyed or injected — the fluorescence is genetic and passed to offspring.

Frequently Asked Questions

Do black skirt tetras nip fins?

They can, especially in small groups or cramped tanks. Keep 6+ together and avoid pairing with long-finned species to minimize this behavior.
metaTitle: Black Skirt Tetra Care Guide: Tank Size, Diet & Tank Mates | World of Aquariums
metaDescription: Complete Black Skirt Tetra care guide. Learn about tank size (15+ gal), water parameters, diet, compatible tank mates, and more.

name: Congo Tetra
diet: Omnivore — flakes, pellets, frozen bloodworms, brine shrimp, daphnia
description: Congo tetras are the showstoppers of the tetra family. Males develop spectacular flowing fins with an iridescent rainbow sheen that shifts from blue to gold to green under aquarium lighting. They are larger than most tetras (up to 3.5 inches) and need a spacious, well-planted tank to display their full beauty. Peaceful but shy — they thrive with calm tank mates.
careGuide:
Tank Setup

  • Tank size: 30 gallons minimum for a school of 6. 55 gallons for 10–12 is ideal to see their full behavior.
  • Lighting: Moderate — their iridescent scales produce the best shimmer under indirect or diffused light
  • Plants: Heavily planted perimeter with open center for schooling. Use plants they can't easily eat — java fern and anubias are good choices. Congo tetras may nibble on tender plants.
  • Substrate: Dark sand dramatically enhances their rainbow iridescence
  • Flow: Gentle — strong current damages their flowing fins

Water Parameters

  • Temperature: 73–82°F (23–28°C)
  • pH: 6.0–7.5 (slightly acidic preferred)
  • GH: 3–12 dGH
  • Ammonia/Nitrite: 0 ppm
  • Nitrate: below 20 ppm

Appearance & Sexual Dimorphism

Males are significantly more colorful than females, with elongated, flowing dorsal and caudal fins that develop as they mature (6–8 months). The iridescent body shimmers through blue, green, gold, and violet depending on the angle and lighting. Females are smaller, less colorful, and have shorter fins.

Diet & Feeding

  • Staple: High-quality flakes or medium-sized pellets
  • Supplements: Frozen bloodworms, brine shrimp, daphnia — these enhance coloration
  • Note: Congo tetras may nibble on soft-leaved plants when underfed. Ensure they get enough food to prevent plant damage.

Tank Mates

Congo tetras are timid despite their size. Pair with calm, peaceful species:

  • Other peaceful tetras (rummy nose, ember)
  • Corydoras, kuhli loaches, bristlenose plecos
  • Dwarf gouramis, angelfish (in 55+ gallon tanks)

Avoid nippy species like serpae tetras and tiger barbs — they will shred the Congo's flowing fins, causing permanent damage and stress.

Frequently Asked Questions

Why are my Congo tetras not colorful?

Males take 6–8 months to develop full coloration and fin length. Stress, poor water quality, inadequate diet, and bright lighting can all suppress color. Dark substrate, soft water, and a varied diet with frozen foods will bring out the best colors.

Do Congo tetras eat plants?

They can nibble on tender plants, especially when underfed. Stick to tough plants like java fern, anubias, and vallisneria. Provide enough food and they'll leave plants alone.
metaTitle: Congo Tetra Care Guide: Tank Size, Diet & Tank Mates | World of Aquariums
metaDescription: Complete Congo Tetra care guide. Learn about tank size (30+ gal), water parameters, diet, compatible tank mates, and more.

name: Cherry Barb
diet: Omnivore — flakes, micro pellets, frozen brine shrimp, daphnia, blanched vegetables
description: Cherry barbs are one of the most peaceful barb species, making them ideal for community tanks. Males develop a deep cherry-red coloration when mature and in breeding condition, while females remain more subdued with brown-pink tones. Unlike their more boisterous relatives like tiger barbs, cherry barbs are gentle, shy, and compatible with even the most delicate tank mates.
careGuide:
Natural Habitat & Origin

Cherry barbs are endemic to Sri Lanka, where they inhabit shaded, heavily vegetated streams and rivers. They are listed as vulnerable in the wild due to habitat loss, but captive-bred populations are thriving. Nearly all cherry barbs in the aquarium trade are captive bred.

Tank Setup

  • Tank size: 10 gallons for 6, 20 gallons for 10–12
  • Plants: Dense planting is essential — cherry barbs are shy and need cover to feel secure. Java moss, cryptocoryne, and vallisneria are excellent choices.
  • Lighting: Subdued to moderate — mimics their shaded natural habitat and brings out the best red coloration in males
  • Substrate: Dark sand or gravel enhances their colors

Water Parameters

  • Temperature: 73–81°F (23–27°C)
  • pH: 6.0–7.0 (slightly acidic preferred)
  • GH: 4–10 dGH

Diet & Feeding

Easy to feed and accept all standard aquarium foods. A varied diet with frozen foods enhances the red coloration in males. Feed small amounts twice daily.

Breeding

Cherry barbs are egg scatterers that are relatively easy to breed. Males display intense red coloration and actively court females. They scatter adhesive eggs among fine-leaved plants or spawning mops. Remove adults after spawning as they will eat the eggs. Eggs hatch in 24–48 hours.

Tank Mates

One of the most peaceful barbs — compatible with virtually all gentle community fish. They are one of the few barb species safe to keep with bettas (in 10+ gallon tanks) and shrimp. Avoid keeping with aggressive or very large fish.

Frequently Asked Questions

Are cherry barbs fin nippers?

No. Unlike tiger barbs, cherry barbs are peaceful and do not nip fins. They are safe with bettas, guppies, and other long-finned fish.

Why isn't my cherry barb red?

Only males turn deep red, and even then it takes maturity (4–6 months) and good conditions. Females stay brownish-pink. Dark substrate, subdued lighting, a varied diet, and low stress all enhance male coloration.
metaTitle: Cherry Barb Care Guide: Tank Size, Diet & Tank Mates | World of Aquariums
metaDescription: Complete Cherry Barb care guide. Learn about tank size (10+ gal), water parameters, diet, compatible tank mates, and more.

name: Tiger Barb
diet: Omnivore — flakes, pellets, frozen bloodworms, brine shrimp, blanched vegetables
description: Tiger barbs are energetic, boldly-striped schooling fish that add constant motion and personality to aquariums. Their orange body with four distinctive black vertical stripes is instantly recognizable. They are notorious fin nippers when kept in small groups, but this behavior is easily managed by keeping 8+ together in a spacious tank.
careGuide:
Natural Habitat & Origin

Tiger barbs originate from the rivers and streams of Sumatra, Borneo, and the Malay Peninsula in Southeast Asia. They inhabit clear, well-oxygenated streams with sandy or rocky substrates. Most pet store specimens are captive bred in Asia and Florida.

Tank Setup

  • Tank size: 20 gallons minimum for a school of 8. 40+ gallons for a school of 12–15 with tank mates.
  • Flow: Moderate — they enjoy swimming against a gentle current
  • Plants: Moderate planting around the perimeter with open swimming space in the center. Tiger barbs are very active swimmers.
  • Substrate: Sand or gravel — not picky

Water Parameters

  • Temperature: 74–82°F (23–28°C)
  • pH: 6.0–7.5
  • GH: 5–15 dGH

Managing Fin Nipping

Tiger barbs are the most commonly cited fin nippers in the hobby. This behavior is almost entirely preventable:

  • Keep 8+ together (10–12 is ideal): In large groups, they establish a hierarchy within the school and direct their energy at each other rather than tank mates
  • Never keep fewer than 6: Small groups lead to extreme nipping behavior
  • Absolutely avoid long-finned tank mates: Bettas, angelfish, fancy guppies, and gouramis will be relentlessly harassed
  • Provide enough space: Cramped conditions increase aggression

Color Varieties

  • Standard tiger barb: Orange body with 4 black bars
  • Green/moss tiger barb: Dark green-black body with iridescent scales
  • Albino tiger barb: Pale gold with cream-colored bars
  • GloFish tiger barb: Fluorescent variants (genetically modified)

All varieties have identical care requirements and can school together.

Diet & Feeding

Tiger barbs are vigorous, greedy eaters that accept all standard foods. Feed a varied diet of flakes, pellets, and frozen foods. Their fast metabolism means they benefit from 2–3 small feedings per day rather than one large one. They will eat until they burst if given the chance — portion control is important.

Tank Mates

Choose robust, fast-moving species with short fins:

  • Good: Other barbs (cherry, rosy, gold), corydoras, bristlenose plecos, platies, mollies, rainbowfish, loaches
  • Acceptable: Black skirt tetras, serpae tetras, swordtails
  • Never: Bettas, angelfish, guppies, gouramis, or any slow-moving/long-finned fish

Frequently Asked Questions

How many tiger barbs should I keep?

Minimum 8, ideally 10–12. This is the single most important factor in managing their behavior. A school of 12 tiger barbs in a 40-gallon tank is active, entertaining, and peaceful toward appropriate tank mates.

Can tiger barbs live with angelfish?

No. Tiger barbs will relentlessly nip the long, flowing fins of angelfish. This is one of the most common compatibility mistakes in the hobby.
metaTitle: Tiger Barb Care Guide: Tank Size, Diet & Tank Mates | World of Aquariums
metaDescription: Complete Tiger Barb care guide. Learn about tank size (20+ gal), water parameters, diet, compatible tank mates, and more.

name: Swordtail
diet: Omnivore — flakes, pellets, blanched vegetables, frozen brine shrimp, spirulina
description: Swordtails are hardy, colorful livebearers named for the distinctive elongated lower tail fin (the 'sword') found on males. They come in many color varieties including red, pineapple, marigold, and tuxedo. Larger than guppies but just as easy to care for, swordtails are excellent community fish that prefer hard, alkaline water.
careGuide:
Tank Setup

  • Tank size: 20 gallons minimum — swordtails are active swimmers that grow larger than most people expect (up to 5.5 inches including the sword)
  • Flow: Moderate — they enjoy swimming against current
  • Plants: Moderate to heavy planting. Live plants provide cover for fry.
  • Lid: Essential — swordtails are excellent jumpers

Water Parameters

  • Temperature: 72–82°F (22–28°C)
  • pH: 7.0–8.4 (they strongly prefer alkaline water)
  • GH: 12–25 dGH (hard water is ideal)

Swordtails do poorly in soft, acidic water. If your tap water is naturally soft, add crushed coral or a mineral supplement.

Breeding

Like all livebearers, swordtails breed prolifically. Females can store sperm for months and produce 20–80 fry every 28 days. If you don't want babies, keep only males — but be aware that multiple males may fight over dominance. A ratio of 1 male to 2–3 females works best.

Sex Change

Swordtails are famous for apparent sex changes — late-developing males can look like females for months before suddenly developing the sword. True female-to-male sex change is debated, but late male maturation is well documented and explains many reports of "females turning into males."

Tank Mates

Excellent community fish compatible with most peaceful species. They pair especially well with other livebearers (platies, mollies) and prefer similar water conditions. Avoid keeping with aggressive species or fin nippers.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can swordtails live with guppies?

Yes, but they may crossbreed (both are livebearers). The offspring are usually sterile and not particularly attractive. If breeding purity matters, keep them separate.
metaTitle: Swordtail Care Guide: Tank Size, Diet & Tank Mates | World of Aquariums
metaDescription: Complete Swordtail care guide. Learn about tank size (20+ gal), water parameters, diet, compatible tank mates, and more.

name: Discus
diet: Carnivore/Omnivore — frozen bloodworms, beef heart mix, high-protein pellets, brine shrimp
description: Discus are widely considered the king of freshwater aquarium fish. Their disk-shaped body, vivid colors, and graceful swimming make them the ultimate showpiece. However, they are among the most demanding freshwater fish to keep, requiring warm, soft, exceptionally clean water, frequent feedings, and a stress-free environment. Discus are not recommended for beginners.
careGuide:
Natural Habitat & Origin

Discus are native to the Amazon River basin, particularly the blackwater and clearwater tributaries in Brazil, Peru, and Colombia. They inhabit warm, soft, acidic water among submerged roots and fallen trees. Wild discus are more muted in color than captive-bred strains, which have been selectively bred for decades to produce brilliant reds, blues, turquoise, pigeon blood, and checkerboard patterns.

Why Discus Are an Advanced Fish

Discus are demanding for several reasons:

  • Temperature: They require 82–88°F — significantly warmer than most tropical fish
  • Water quality: Extremely sensitive to ammonia, nitrite, and nitrate. Many keepers perform daily 50% water changes.
  • Diet: Picky eaters that need high-protein foods, often multiple feedings per day
  • Stress sensitivity: Stressed discus turn dark, stop eating, and are susceptible to disease
  • Cost: Quality discus cost $30–$150+ each, and you need at least 5

Tank Setup

  • Tank size: 55 gallons for 5 discus, 75+ for 6–8. Taller tanks are preferred since discus are vertically oriented.
  • Temperature: 82–88°F — a reliable heater (or two for redundancy) is critical
  • Substrate: Many serious discus keepers use bare-bottom tanks for easier cleaning. If using substrate, fine sand is preferred.
  • Filtration: Heavy filtration — canister filters rated for 2–3x the tank volume
  • Plants: Optional but beautiful. Choose heat-tolerant species: anubias, java fern, amazon swords, vallisneria. Avoid CO2-dependent plants unless you're experienced.
  • Driftwood: Releases tannins that help acidify and soften water naturally
  • Lighting: Moderate — avoid harsh, direct light

Water Parameters

  • Temperature: 82–88°F (28–31°C) — non-negotiable
  • pH: 5.5–7.0 (soft, acidic water)
  • GH: 1–4 dGH (very soft)
  • Ammonia/Nitrite: 0 ppm (absolutely zero tolerance)
  • Nitrate: below 10 ppm (ideally below 5 ppm)
  • TDS: 50–150 ppm

Water changes are the most critical aspect of discus keeping. Many experienced keepers perform 50% daily water changes for growing juveniles and 30–50% water changes 3–5 times per week for adults. RO/DI water or remineralized RO water is often necessary depending on your tap water.

Diet & Feeding

  • Juveniles: Feed 5–6 times per day with high-protein foods to promote growth. Frozen bloodworms, beef heart mix, and Hikari Discus Bio-Gold are staples.
  • Adults: Feed 2–3 times per day. Varied diet of frozen bloodworms, brine shrimp, high-protein pellets, and occasional beef heart.
  • Avoid: Tubifex worms (parasite risk), low-quality flakes

Discus are slow, deliberate eaters. Ensure they get food before faster tank mates consume it all. Remove uneaten food promptly — it fouls water quickly at discus temperatures.

Popular Varieties

  • Blue Diamond: Solid metallic blue. One of the most popular strains.
  • Pigeon Blood: White/cream body with red spots and markings
  • Turquoise: Blue-green base with red/brown striations
  • Red Melon: Solid orange-red, highly sought after
  • Checkerboard: Red and blue checkerboard pattern
  • Snakeskin: Fine spotted/striped pattern
  • Wild-caught: Heckel, brown, green, and blue wild forms. More demanding but rewarding for experienced keepers.

Tank Mates

The warm temperature requirement limits compatible species:

  • Best: Cardinal tetras, rummy nose tetras (both tolerate warm water and look stunning with discus)
  • Good: Sterbai corydoras (heat-tolerant cory species), bristlenose plecos, otocinclus
  • Shrimp: Amano shrimp and cherry shrimp can work but may be eaten by large discus
  • Avoid: Any aggressive or fast-moving species that will outcompete discus for food, plus anything that requires cooler water

Common Diseases

  • Hexamita (hole-in-the-head): Pitting/lesions on the head. Caused by internal parasites exacerbated by stress and poor water quality. Treat with metronidazole.
  • Gill flukes: Rapid breathing, scratching on surfaces. Treat with praziquantel.
  • Internal parasites: Common in wild-caught and imported discus. Prophylactic deworming with General Cure is recommended for new arrivals.
  • Stress darkening: Discus turn dark brown/black when stressed. Identify and remove the stressor (water quality, aggression, illness).

Frequently Asked Questions

Are discus hard to keep?

Yes. They are one of the most demanding freshwater fish. They require pristine water, warm temperatures, frequent feedings, and a stress-free environment. They are not recommended for anyone who hasn't successfully kept other tropical fish for at least a year.

How many discus should I keep?

At least 5. Discus are social and establish hierarchies. Groups smaller than 5 result in excessive bullying of the lowest-ranked fish. Odd numbers (5, 7) tend to distribute aggression better than even numbers.

Can I keep discus in a planted tank?

Yes, but it's challenging. The high temperature (84–86°F) limits plant choices and accelerates algae growth. Low-tech plants like anubias, java fern, and amazon swords do well. Avoid high-demand plants that need CO2 and intense lighting unless you're very experienced.
metaTitle: Discus Care Guide: Tank Size, Diet & Tank Mates | World of Aquariums
metaDescription: Complete Discus care guide. Learn about tank size (55+ gal), water parameters, diet, compatible tank mates, and more.

name: German Blue Ram
diet: Omnivore — micro pellets, frozen bloodworms, brine shrimp, daphnia
description: German blue rams are one of the most beautiful dwarf cichlids, with iridescent blue and gold coloration, a distinctive black spot, and red-highlighted fins. Despite being cichlids, they are remarkably peaceful and make excellent centerpiece fish for planted community tanks. They do require warm, soft water and are more sensitive than typical community fish.
careGuide:
Natural Habitat & Origin

Rams originate from the Orinoco River basin in Venezuela and Colombia, inhabiting warm, shallow pools with sandy bottoms and scattered vegetation. The warm temperature of their natural habitat (82–86°F) is a non-negotiable requirement in captivity — many rams die prematurely because keepers maintain them at standard tropical temperatures (76–78°F) instead of the warmer range they need.

Tank Setup

  • Tank size: 20 gallons for a pair, 30+ for a pair with community fish
  • Temperature: 78–86°F — they thrive best at 80–84°F, warmer than most community fish
  • Substrate: Fine sand — rams sift through sand looking for food, which is natural and entertaining behavior
  • Plants: Well-planted tank with open sandy areas for foraging
  • Caves: Flat rocks, coconut caves, or small ceramic caves for potential spawning sites

Water Parameters

  • Temperature: 78–86°F (26–30°C)
  • pH: 5.5–7.0 (soft, acidic water strongly preferred)
  • GH: 1–8 dGH
  • Ammonia/Nitrite: 0 ppm
  • Nitrate: below 10 ppm

Rams are sensitive to water quality. They are often the first fish in a tank to show signs of stress from elevated nitrate or insufficient water changes. Pristine water is essential.

Varieties

  • German Blue Ram: The classic variety — blue and gold with red fins
  • Electric Blue Ram: Selectively bred for solid iridescent blue. Stunning but slightly more delicate.
  • Gold Ram: Yellow-gold body, less common
  • Balloon Ram: Shorter, rounder body. More prone to health issues due to compressed organs.
  • Long-fin Ram: Extended fins, beautiful but slightly more fragile

Diet & Feeding

  • Staple: High-quality micro pellets (Northfin, Hikari)
  • Supplements: Frozen bloodworms, brine shrimp, daphnia — 3–4 times per week
  • Note: Rams are slow, deliberate eaters. If kept with fast feeders like tetras, target-feed them directly with a turkey baster or feeding pipette.

Tank Mates

One of the most peaceful cichlids — they work beautifully in planted community tanks:

  • Tetras (cardinal, rummy nose, ember — all tolerate warm water)
  • Corydoras (sterbai corys for the warm temperature)
  • Otocinclus, kuhli loaches, bristlenose plecos
  • Cherry shrimp (may eat very small shrimp fry but generally safe)

Avoid keeping with aggressive fish or other dwarf cichlids in small tanks (they will fight over territory).

Breeding

Rams are substrate spawners that lay eggs on flat rocks, broad leaves, or inside caves. They form monogamous pairs and both parents guard the eggs and fry. Breeding is rewarding but first-time parents often eat their eggs — be patient, as they improve with each attempt.

Common Issues

  • Short lifespan: Pet store rams often only live 1–2 years due to inbreeding, hormonal treatment during farming, and stress from poor conditions. Rams from reputable breeders live significantly longer (3–4 years).
  • Temperature sensitivity: Many die prematurely from being kept too cool. Keep at 80°F+ for best health.
  • Hexamita: Internal parasite that causes white, stringy feces and wasting. Treat with metronidazole. Common in stressed or newly purchased rams.

Frequently Asked Questions

Why do my German blue rams keep dying?

The three most common causes: 1) Temperature too low (keep at 80–84°F), 2) Poor water quality (keep nitrate below 10 ppm), 3) Poor-quality stock from mass breeders. Buy from reputable breeders or local fish stores that source quality stock.

Can I keep two male rams together?

In a 30+ gallon tank with plenty of visual barriers and caves, two males can coexist. In smaller tanks, they will fight constantly. A male-female pair is the easiest combination.
metaTitle: German Blue Ram Care Guide: Tank Size, Diet & Tank Mates | World of Aquariums
metaDescription: Complete German Blue Ram care guide. Learn about tank size (20+ gal), water parameters, diet, compatible tank mates, and more.

name: Jack Dempsey Cichlid
diet: Carnivore — pellets, frozen krill, earthworms, feeder shrimp, bloodworms
description: Named after the legendary boxer, Jack Dempsey cichlids are large, territorial Central American cichlids with iridescent blue-green spangles across their dark body. They are aggressive, intelligent, and interactive — many owners report them coming to the glass to greet them. The electric blue Jack Dempsey variant is significantly less aggressive and more colorful.
careGuide:
Tank Setup

  • Tank size: 55 gallons minimum for a single Jack Dempsey. 75+ for a pair or with tank mates.
  • Substrate: Sand — they dig extensively
  • Decor: Rocks, driftwood, and caves for territory establishment. Secure decorations well — JDs rearrange their tanks.
  • Plants: They will uproot most plants. Use anubias and java fern attached to rocks/driftwood if you want live plants.
  • Filtration: Heavy-duty canister filter — JDs are messy eaters and produce significant waste

Water Parameters

  • Temperature: 72–86°F (22–30°C)
  • pH: 6.5–8.0
  • GH: 8–15 dGH

Electric Blue Jack Dempsey

The electric blue variant (EBJD) is a naturally occurring mutation that produces stunning solid electric blue coloration. EBJDs are significantly less aggressive than the standard form, making them easier community fish. However, they are also more delicate and prone to health issues. They grow slightly smaller (6–8 inches vs 8–10 for standard).

Managing Aggression

  • Provide a large tank (75+ gallons) with multiple territories and visual barriers
  • Only keep with other large, robust fish that can hold their own
  • JDs are most aggressive during breeding — separate pairs if breeding becomes too disruptive
  • A single JD in a large tank is the easiest setup for most keepers

Tank Mates

Only with other robust, similarly-sized fish:

  • Other Central/South American cichlids of similar size (firemouths, salvini, severum)
  • Oscars (in 125+ gallon tanks)
  • Large plecos (common pleco, bristlenose in a 75+ gallon)
  • Silver dollars, large barbs

Never keep with any small fish — they will be eaten.

Frequently Asked Questions

How big do Jack Dempseys get?

Standard JDs reach 8–10 inches. Electric blues reach 6–8 inches. They grow relatively slowly, reaching full size in 2–3 years.
metaTitle: Jack Dempsey Cichlid Care Guide: Tank Size, Diet & Tank Mates | World of Aquariums
metaDescription: Complete Jack Dempsey Cichlid care guide. Learn about tank size (55+ gal), water parameters, diet, compatible tank mates, and more.

name: Firemouth Cichlid
diet: Omnivore — pellets, flakes, frozen bloodworms, brine shrimp, blanched vegetables
description: Firemouth cichlids are one of the most beginner-friendly Central American cichlids. Named for their striking red/orange coloration on the throat and belly, they are less aggressive than most cichlids of their size. When threatened, they flare their gill covers to display their bright red throat — a bluffing display that is far more bark than bite.
careGuide:
Tank Setup

  • Tank size: 30 gallons for a single fish, 55+ for a pair or with tank mates
  • Substrate: Sand — firemouths are prolific diggers and sifters
  • Caves: Multiple caves and hiding spots for territory. Terracotta pots, stacked rocks, and driftwood work well.
  • Plants: Can work with robust plants (anubias, java fern) attached to hardscape. They will uproot anything planted in substrate.

Water Parameters

  • Temperature: 75–86°F (24–30°C)
  • pH: 6.5–8.0
  • GH: 8–15 dGH

Behavior & Aggression

Firemouths are among the least aggressive Central American cichlids. Their famous gill-flaring display is mostly bluff — they rarely follow through with actual attacks. However, they become significantly more aggressive when:

  • Breeding (both parents aggressively defend eggs and fry)
  • Kept in too-small tanks
  • Paired with much smaller or very passive fish

Tank Mates

  • Good: Other similarly-sized Central American cichlids (in 75+ gallon tanks), large barbs, swordtails, robust catfish
  • Avoid: Small tetras, guppies, shrimp, or anything they can fit in their mouth

Frequently Asked Questions

Are firemouth cichlids aggressive?

Mildly. They are one of the least aggressive Central American cichlids. Their gill-flaring display is mostly bluff. They're a great "gateway" cichlid for keepers who want to try cichlids without extreme aggression.
metaTitle: Firemouth Cichlid Care Guide: Tank Size, Diet & Tank Mates | World of Aquariums
metaDescription: Complete Firemouth Cichlid care guide. Learn about tank size (30+ gal), water parameters, diet, compatible tank mates, and more.

name: Convict Cichlid
diet: Omnivore — pellets, flakes, frozen foods, blanched vegetables, earthworms
description: Convict cichlids are tough, prolific Central American cichlids with distinctive black and white vertical stripes — earning them the 'convict' name. They are extremely hardy, breed readily (almost uncontrollably), and are highly aggressive for their size, especially when protecting fry. They are excellent for beginners who want to keep cichlids but should not be mixed with peaceful community fish.
careGuide:
Tank Setup

  • Tank size: 30 gallons for a single fish, 55+ for a pair (they WILL breed)
  • Caves: Multiple caves and territory markers. Convicts are cave spawners and will aggressively defend their chosen cave.
  • Substrate: Sand or gravel — they dig constantly
  • Plants: They will destroy most plants. Use only hardy, attached plants if any.

Water Parameters

  • Temperature: 72–82°F
  • pH: 6.5–8.0
  • GH: 8–20 dGH

Convicts are among the hardiest freshwater fish. They tolerate a wide range of water conditions and are nearly impossible to kill through water parameter issues alone.

Breeding — The Convict Problem

Convict cichlids are legendary breeders. A male-female pair will breed every 3–4 weeks, producing 100–300 fry each time. Both parents are exceptional, ferociously protective parents that will attack anything — including your hand — that comes near their fry.

The "convict problem" is that most fishkeepers cannot rehome the constant stream of babies. Consider carefully before keeping a pair. Options include:

  • Keeping only one sex (though sexing juveniles is difficult)
  • Keeping a single convict (they have plenty of personality solo)
  • Adding egg-eating tank mates that control population naturally

Aggression Management

Convicts punch way above their weight class. A 4-inch convict will fearlessly attack fish twice its size. Their aggression escalates dramatically when:

  • Breeding or guarding fry (both parents become extremely aggressive)
  • Tank is too small
  • Paired with timid fish that can't defend themselves

Tank Mates

Only with other robust, aggressive fish of similar or larger size:

  • Other Central American cichlids (firemouths, Jack Dempseys in large tanks)
  • Large, armored catfish (common pleco, bristlenose)
  • Silver dollars, large barbs

Never with any small or peaceful species.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can convict cichlids live alone?

Yes, and this is often the best option. A single convict in a 30-gallon tank displays all their personality without the complications of constant breeding.

How do I stop my convicts from breeding?

Keep only one sex, keep a single fish, or add robust egg-eating tank mates. Once a pair forms, they will breed continuously and there is no way to stop them short of separation.
metaTitle: Convict Cichlid Care Guide: Tank Size, Diet & Tank Mates | World of Aquariums
metaDescription: Complete Convict Cichlid care guide. Learn about tank size (30+ gal), water parameters, diet, compatible tank mates, and more.

name: Silver Dollar Fish
diet: Herbivore — blanched vegetables, spirulina flakes, algae wafers, occasional frozen foods
description: Silver dollars are large, disc-shaped, peaceful schooling fish related to piranhas — but entirely herbivorous. Their shimmering silver body catches light beautifully in motion. They are excellent dither fish for large cichlid tanks because their calm, open-water swimming behavior helps reduce cichlid aggression. Warning: they will eat every live plant in your tank.
careGuide:
Tank Setup

  • Tank size: 55 gallons minimum for a school of 5. 75+ is better — they are active swimmers that grow to 6 inches.
  • Plants: DO NOT USE LIVE PLANTS. Silver dollars are voracious herbivores that will eat every plant within days. Use artificial plants or leave the tank unplanted.
  • Decor: Driftwood, rocks, and artificial plants for cover
  • Lid: Essential — silver dollars are skittish and jump when startled

Water Parameters

  • Temperature: 75–82°F
  • pH: 5.5–7.5 (slightly acidic preferred)
  • GH: 4–15 dGH

Diet & Feeding

Primarily herbivorous — one of the few truly plant-eating aquarium fish:

  • Staple: Spirulina flakes, algae wafers
  • Vegetables: Blanched lettuce, spinach, peas, zucchini — they devour these
  • Supplements: Occasional frozen bloodworms or brine shrimp for protein

Tank Mates

Despite being related to piranhas, silver dollars are completely peaceful. Their large size and open-water swimming makes them excellent companions for large cichlids — their calm presence as "dither fish" actually reduces cichlid aggression. Compatible with oscars, Jack Dempseys, firemouths, large barbs, and other robust species.

Frequently Asked Questions

Are silver dollars related to piranhas?

Yes — they are in the same family (Serrasalmidae). However, silver dollars are entirely herbivorous and completely harmless. Think of them as the "vegetarian piranhas."

Will silver dollars eat my plants?

Absolutely. Every single one. They are among the most destructive plant-eaters in the freshwater hobby. Do not put live plants in a silver dollar tank.
metaTitle: Silver Dollar Fish Care Guide: Tank Size, Diet & Tank Mates | World of Aquariums
metaDescription: Complete Silver Dollar Fish care guide. Learn about tank size (55+ gal), water parameters, diet, compatible tank mates, and more.

name: Boesemani Rainbowfish
diet: Omnivore — flakes, pellets, frozen brine shrimp, daphnia, bloodworms
description: Boesemani rainbowfish are among the most colorful freshwater fish available. Males display a stunning gradient from steel blue on the front half to bright orange-gold on the back half. They are active, hardy schooling fish that reach a respectable 4–5 inches and look absolutely spectacular in schools of 8+ under good lighting.
careGuide:
Natural Habitat & Origin

Boesemani rainbowfish are native to Lake Ayamaru and nearby lakes in West Papua, Indonesia. They are listed as endangered in the wild due to habitat degradation. Nearly all aquarium specimens are captive bred.

Tank Setup

  • Tank size: 30 gallons minimum for a school of 6, 55+ for 8–12
  • Flow: Moderate — they enjoy swimming against gentle current
  • Plants: Moderate planting with plenty of open swimming space
  • Lighting: Good lighting is essential to appreciate their colors. Their iridescent scales shimmer under white or full-spectrum LEDs.

Water Parameters

  • Temperature: 77–86°F (25–30°C)
  • pH: 7.0–8.0 (slightly alkaline preferred)
  • GH: 10–20 dGH (moderately hard water)

Coloration

Pet store rainbowfish are often disappointing — juvenile and stressed fish show pale, washed-out colors. Don't judge the species by what you see at the store. Males develop their full blue-to-orange gradient at 6–12 months in a well-maintained tank. Morning light brings out the most intense coloration, and males display even brighter colors when competing for female attention.

Tank Mates

Hardy, active community fish. They pair well with other robust species of similar size. Their slightly alkaline, hard water preference makes them good companions for livebearers (platies, mollies, swordtails) and larger barbs. Too fast and boisterous for very small or timid fish like neon tetras.

Frequently Asked Questions

Why are my rainbowfish not colorful?

Juveniles are pale — color develops with maturity (6–12 months). Stress, poor water quality, cold temperatures, and inadequate diet also suppress color. Males kept with females display the most vibrant coloration.
metaTitle: Boesemani Rainbowfish Care Guide: Tank Size, Diet & Tank Mates | World of Aquariums
metaDescription: Complete Boesemani Rainbowfish care guide. Learn about tank size (30+ gal), water parameters, diet, compatible tank mates, and more.

name: Clown Pleco
diet: Herbivore/Wood eater — driftwood, blanched vegetables, algae wafers
description: Clown plecos are beautiful, small plecos with striking orange and black striped patterns. At only 3.5 inches max size, they are perfect for 20-gallon and larger community tanks. Unlike many pleco species, they are primarily wood eaters rather than algae eaters — driftwood is an essential part of their setup. They are shy, nocturnal, and make excellent additions to planted tanks.
careGuide:
Tank Setup

  • Tank size: 20 gallons minimum
  • Driftwood: Absolutely essential — clown plecos are wood eaters. They rasp on driftwood constantly and derive nutrition from the wood fibers. Multiple pieces of driftwood should be provided.
  • Hiding spots: Caves, crevices in driftwood, coconut shells. They are nocturnal and need dark hiding spots for daytime.
  • Substrate: Sand or fine gravel

Wood-Eating Behavior

Clown plecos are xylivores — organisms that eat wood. Unlike bristlenose plecos that primarily eat algae, clown plecos depend on driftwood for digestion and nutrition. A tank without driftwood will result in a malnourished, unhealthy clown pleco. Malaysian driftwood, mopani, and manzanita are all good choices.

Are They Good Algae Eaters?

Not particularly. While they may graze on some soft algae, they are primarily wood eaters and will not keep your tank algae-free. If you need algae control, get a bristlenose pleco, nerite snails, or otocinclus instead.

Water Parameters

  • Temperature: 73–82°F
  • pH: 6.5–7.5
  • GH: 5–15 dGH

Diet & Feeding

  • Primary: Driftwood (always available in the tank)
  • Supplements: Blanched zucchini, cucumber, sweet potato — drop in at lights-out
  • Pellets: Algae wafers, Repashy Morning Wood (specifically formulated for wood-eating plecos)

Frequently Asked Questions

How big do clown plecos get?

3–3.5 inches. They are one of the smallest pleco species and are perfectly suited for 20-gallon tanks.

Do clown plecos eat algae?

Minimally. They primarily eat wood. Get a bristlenose pleco or nerite snails if you need algae control.
metaTitle: Clown Pleco Care Guide: Tank Size, Diet & Tank Mates | World of Aquariums
metaDescription: Complete Clown Pleco care guide. Learn about tank size (20+ gal), water parameters, diet, compatible tank mates, and more.

name: Pygmy Corydoras
diet: Omnivore — micro pellets, crushed flakes, frozen cyclops, baby brine shrimp
description: Pygmy corydoras are tiny, adorable catfish that max out at just 1 inch. Unlike other corys that stay on the bottom, pygmy corys are unique in that they frequently swim in the mid-water column, hovering in groups like tiny helicopters. They are perfect for nano tanks and shrimp tanks, and their micro-schooling behavior is endlessly entertaining.
careGuide:
Tank Setup

  • Tank size: 10 gallons for a school of 10–12
  • Substrate: Fine sand is ideal — their tiny barbels are delicate
  • Plants: Heavily planted with resting spots near the surface (broad-leaved plants like anubias). Pygmy corys perch on leaves and driftwood throughout the water column.
  • Flow: Gentle — they are tiny and get pushed around by strong current

Unique Behavior

Unlike regular corydoras that are primarily bottom dwellers, pygmy corys spend much of their time in the mid-water column, hovering in groups. They dart to the surface for gulps of air (normal cory behavior), perch on plant leaves, and school in loose formations. A group of 15+ pygmy corys in a planted nano tank is absolutely charming.

Water Parameters

  • Temperature: 72–79°F
  • pH: 6.0–7.5
  • GH: 2–10 dGH

Diet & Feeding

Their tiny mouths require appropriately sized food:

  • Crushed high-quality flakes or micro pellets
  • Frozen baby brine shrimp, cyclops, daphnia
  • They also graze constantly on biofilm in mature tanks

Tank Mates

Only with very small, gentle species. Their tiny size makes them vulnerable to being eaten by anything with a big enough mouth. Excellent companions for cherry shrimp, ember tetras, endlers, and bettas (in larger tanks).

Frequently Asked Questions

How many pygmy corydoras should I keep?

At least 10, ideally 15+. In large groups they display confident, active behavior throughout the tank. In small groups they hide and show stress.

Pygmy cory vs habrosus vs hastatus?

All three are "dwarf" corys. Pygmaeus swims mid-water and schools loosely. Habrosus is slightly larger and stays more on the bottom. Hastatus is the smallest and hovers in tight schools near the bottom. All have similar care requirements.
metaTitle: Pygmy Corydoras Care Guide: Tank Size, Diet & Tank Mates | World of Aquariums
metaDescription: Complete Pygmy Corydoras care guide. Learn about tank size (10+ gal), water parameters, diet, compatible tank mates, and more.

name: Pearl Gourami
diet: Omnivore — flakes, pellets, frozen bloodworms, brine shrimp, blanched vegetables
description: Pearl gouramis are widely considered the most beautiful gourami species, with a body covered in iridescent pearl-like spots over a brown-silver base. Males develop a stunning red-orange chest and throat during breeding displays. They are one of the most peaceful gouramis, making them excellent centerpiece fish for community tanks.
careGuide:
Tank Setup

  • Tank size: 30 gallons minimum for a single or pair
  • Plants: Heavily planted with floating plants — pearl gouramis are labyrinth fish that breathe air from the surface. Floating plants provide security and bubble nest building sites.
  • Flow: Gentle — like all labyrinth fish, they prefer calm water
  • Hiding spots: Dense vegetation and driftwood provide necessary cover

Water Parameters

  • Temperature: 77–82°F (25–28°C)
  • pH: 5.5–7.5 (slightly acidic preferred)
  • GH: 2–15 dGH

Labyrinth Organ

Like bettas, pearl gouramis have a labyrinth organ that allows them to breathe atmospheric air. You'll see them regularly visit the surface to gulp air — this is completely normal. Ensure there is always access to the surface (no fully covered water) and that the air above the water is warm (keep the tank lidded so cold air doesn't cause respiratory stress).

Diet & Feeding

Easy to feed. Accept virtually all standard aquarium foods. A varied diet with frozen foods enhances their pearl iridescence. They are also excellent surface feeders and may eat small pest insects that land on the water.

Tank Mates

One of the most peaceful gouramis. Pairs well with virtually all community fish. Avoid keeping with bettas (related species often conflict) or aggressive fin nippers. Multiple males may spar mildly but rarely cause damage in a 30+ gallon tank.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can pearl gouramis live with bettas?

Not recommended. Both are labyrinth fish and males may see each other as rivals. The pearl gourami's similar body shape can trigger aggression from a male betta.

How can I tell male from female?

Males develop a red-orange throat and chest, have a longer, more pointed dorsal fin, and are generally more colorful. Females are rounder and lack the red coloration.
metaTitle: Pearl Gourami Care Guide: Tank Size, Diet & Tank Mates | World of Aquariums
metaDescription: Complete Pearl Gourami care guide. Learn about tank size (30+ gal), water parameters, diet, compatible tank mates, and more.

name: Honey Gourami
diet: Omnivore — micro pellets, flakes, frozen brine shrimp, daphnia
description: Honey gouramis are the gentlest gourami species — even more peaceful than their larger dwarf gourami relatives. Males display a warm honey-gold coloration that deepens to rich amber when breeding. At only 2 inches, they are perfect for nano community tanks. They are also significantly hardier than dwarf gouramis, which suffer from the iridovirus (DGIV).
careGuide:
Honey Gourami vs Dwarf Gourami

Many fishkeepers confuse honey gouramis with dwarf gouramis, but they are different species with important differences:

  • Size: Honey gouramis are smaller (2 inches vs 3.5 inches)
  • Health: Honey gouramis are not affected by Dwarf Gourami Iridovirus (DGIV), a devastating disease that affects the majority of mass-bred dwarf gouramis
  • Temperament: Even more peaceful than dwarf gouramis
  • Color: Honey gouramis are gold/amber; dwarf gouramis are blue and red striped

If you want a small gourami, honey gouramis are the safer, hardier choice.

Tank Setup

  • Tank size: 10 gallons minimum
  • Plants: Heavily planted with floating plants — provides security and bubble nest sites
  • Flow: Very gentle — they are small and prefer still to barely moving water

Water Parameters

  • Temperature: 74–82°F
  • pH: 6.0–7.5
  • GH: 4–10 dGH

Diet & Feeding

Feed small foods appropriate for their tiny mouth — micro pellets, crushed flakes, frozen daphnia, baby brine shrimp. They are slow, deliberate eaters. In a community tank, ensure they get food before faster species consume it all.

Tank Mates

Only with small, peaceful species. Honey gouramis are shy and will be outcompeted and stressed by large or active fish. Ideal companions include ember tetras, pygmy corydoras, small rasboras, cherry shrimp, and snails. Avoid bettas — both are labyrinth fish and may conflict.

Frequently Asked Questions

How many honey gouramis can I keep together?

A single honey gourami or a male-female pair works well in 10–15 gallons. In 20+ gallon tanks, you can keep a trio (1 male, 2 females) or small group. Multiple males may spar mildly but rarely cause harm if there's enough space and cover.
metaTitle: Honey Gourami Care Guide: Tank Size, Diet & Tank Mates | World of Aquariums
metaDescription: Complete Honey Gourami care guide. Learn about tank size (10+ gal), water parameters, diet, compatible tank mates, and more.

name: Ocellaris Clownfish
diet: Omnivore — marine pellets, frozen mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, marine flakes
description: The ocellaris clownfish — star of Finding Nemo — is the most popular saltwater aquarium fish and one of the best choices for beginner reef keepers. Captive-bred specimens are hardy, readily available, and don't require an anemone host (though they will happily adopt one). They are one of the few marine fish that breed regularly in home aquariums.
careGuide:
Natural Habitat

Ocellaris clownfish are found throughout the Indo-Pacific, from the Andaman Sea to Western Australia. In the wild, they live in symbiotic relationships with host anemones — the clown provides food scraps to the anemone, and the anemone's stinging tentacles protect the clown from predators. A mucus coating on the clownfish prevents them from being stung.

Why Buy Captive-Bred

Always choose captive-bred (CB) clownfish over wild-caught. Captive-bred clowns are:

  • Hardier and better acclimated to aquarium conditions
  • Less susceptible to disease
  • Already eating prepared foods
  • Environmentally sustainable — no impact on wild reefs
  • Available in designer varieties (snowflake, black ice, mocha, etc.)

Tank Setup

  • Tank size: 20 gallons minimum for a pair. Clownfish don't need large tanks — they naturally stay close to their host.
  • Filtration: Standard reef tank setup — live rock, protein skimmer, and a return pump or powerhead
  • Anemone: NOT required. Captive-bred clowns will host in corals (torch, hammer, frogspawn), macroalgae, or even powerheads. If you want to provide an anemone, choose a Bubble Tip Anemone (BTA) — the easiest to keep.
  • Rock work: Provide some structure but clowns don't need extensive caves

Water Parameters

  • Temperature: 75–82°F
  • pH: 8.0–8.4
  • Salinity: 1.024–1.026 SG
  • Ammonia/Nitrite: 0 ppm
  • Nitrate: below 20 ppm (below 10 for reef)
  • Calcium: 400–450 ppm
  • Alkalinity: 8–12 dKH

Diet & Feeding

  • Staple: High-quality marine pellets (TDO Chroma Boost, Hikari Marine)
  • Supplements: Frozen mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, marine algae
  • Frequency: 2–3 times daily in small amounts

Pairing & Social Behavior

Clownfish are protandrous hermaphrodites — all are born male, and the dominant fish in a pair becomes female. When keeping two clownfish:

  • Start with two juvenile captive-bred clowns of different sizes — the larger one will become female
  • Never add a third clownfish to an established pair
  • A bonded pair can be kept for 15+ years

Designer Varieties

  • Snowflake: Irregular white bands
  • Black Ice: Dark body with white bands
  • Mocha/Chocolate: Brown-toned variant
  • Platinum: Entirely white
  • Naked: No stripes at all

All designer varieties have identical care requirements to standard ocellaris.

Tank Mates

Compatible with most peaceful reef fish:

  • Royal grammas, firefish gobies, blennies, cardinalfish
  • Tangs and wrasses (in larger tanks)
  • All invertebrates (corals, shrimp, snails — reef safe)

Avoid other clownfish species (they will fight) and aggressive damsels.

Frequently Asked Questions

Do clownfish need an anemone?

No. Captive-bred clownfish thrive without anemones. Many will host in coral alternatives or simply claim a corner of the tank. Anemones are actually harder to keep than clownfish and are not recommended for beginners.

How long do clownfish live?

10–15 years in well-maintained aquariums. Some have been documented living 20+ years.

Can I keep just one clownfish?

Yes. A single clownfish is perfectly happy. They don't need a mate to thrive.
metaTitle: Ocellaris Clownfish Care Guide: Tank Size, Diet & Tank Mates | World of Aquariums
metaDescription: Complete Ocellaris Clownfish care guide. Learn about tank size (20+ gal), water parameters, diet, compatible tank mates, and more.

name: Royal Gramma
diet: Carnivore — frozen mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, marine pellets
description: The royal gramma is one of the most stunning and beginner-friendly saltwater fish. Its front half is vivid purple transitioning to bright yellow on the back — a color combination unmatched in the marine world. They are hardy, disease-resistant, reef-safe, and stay small. Often considered one of the top 3 beginner saltwater fish alongside clownfish and firefish.
careGuide:
Tank Setup

  • Tank size: 20 gallons minimum
  • Rock work: Essential — royal grammas are cave dwellers. They spend much of their time hovering near or inside small caves and overhangs, often swimming upside down along the ceiling of their cave.
  • Multiple hiding spots: Provide at least 2–3 cave options

Water Parameters

  • Temperature: 75–80°F
  • pH: 8.1–8.4
  • Salinity: 1.024–1.026 SG
  • Ammonia/Nitrite: 0 ppm

Behavior

Royal grammas are territorial about their chosen cave but otherwise peaceful. They have the unique habit of swimming upside down along the underside of rock overhangs — this is completely normal cave-dwelling behavior, not a sign of illness. They are hardy and resistant to most common marine diseases.

Tank Mates

Excellent reef community fish. Compatible with clownfish, firefish, blennies, cardinalfish, and most peaceful marine species. Avoid keeping two royal grammas together unless in a very large tank (75+ gallons) — they are territorial toward their own species.

Frequently Asked Questions

Why does my royal gramma swim upside down?

This is completely normal. Royal grammas naturally swim along the undersides of rock overhangs in their caves. It's not a sign of swim bladder disease.

Is the royal gramma reef safe?

Yes. Completely reef safe — will not harm corals, anemones, or invertebrates.
metaTitle: Royal Gramma Care Guide: Tank Size, Diet & Tank Mates | World of Aquariums
metaDescription: Complete Royal Gramma care guide. Learn about tank size (20+ gal), water parameters, diet, compatible tank mates, and more.

name: Firefish Goby
diet: Carnivore — frozen mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, marine pellets
description: Firefish gobies are graceful, flame-colored marine fish that hover above the rock work like tiny, living flames. Their elongated dorsal spine and gradient from white to red-orange makes them visually striking. They are peaceful, hardy, and reef-safe, making them ideal for beginner marine aquariums. They require a tight-fitting lid as they are notorious jumpers.
careGuide:
Tank Setup

  • Tank size: 20 gallons minimum
  • Lid: ABSOLUTELY ESSENTIAL. Firefish are the #1 jumpers in the marine hobby. Even small gaps in lids will result in carpet surfing. Use mesh screen or egg crate if you can't fully seal the top.
  • Rock work: Provide rubble zones and small caves for bolt holes — firefish dart into hiding when startled
  • Flow: Moderate — they hover in the current

Water Parameters

  • Temperature: 75–80°F
  • pH: 8.1–8.4
  • Salinity: 1.024–1.026 SG

Behavior

Firefish are shy, hovering fish that spend their time suspended in the water column near their bolt hole. When threatened, they dart headfirst into a crevice with incredible speed. They can be slow to acclimate to a new tank and may hide for days or weeks after introduction. Be patient — they will eventually come out.

Tank Mates

Only with peaceful species. Firefish are easily bullied and will stop eating if harassed. Good companions include clownfish, royal grammas (with caution — grammas can be territorial), gobies, and cardinalfish. Never keep two firefish together unless they are an established pair — they are territorial toward their own species.

Frequently Asked Questions

Why is my firefish hiding?

New firefish commonly hide for 1–3 weeks after introduction. This is normal. Ensure there are no aggressive tank mates bullying them. They will gradually gain confidence and spend more time in the open.

Can I keep two firefish together?

Only as an established mated pair. Two random firefish will fight, with the loser often jumping out of the tank or being harassed to death.
metaTitle: Firefish Goby Care Guide: Tank Size, Diet & Tank Mates | World of Aquariums
metaDescription: Complete Firefish Goby care guide. Learn about tank size (20+ gal), water parameters, diet, compatible tank mates, and more.

name: Yellow Tang
diet: Herbivore — nori sheets, marine algae, spirulina, mysis shrimp
description: The yellow tang is the most iconic reef tank fish — a solid lemon-yellow disc that constantly grazes algae across the rock work. They are active, hardy, and excellent algae controllers for reef tanks. However, they need large tanks (75+ gallons minimum), can be aggressive toward similar-shaped fish, and have become significantly more expensive following collection bans in Hawaii.
careGuide:
Tank Setup

  • Tank size: 75 gallons minimum — yellow tangs are active swimmers that need horizontal swimming space. A 4-foot tank is the minimum length.
  • Rock work: Lots of live rock for grazing. Yellow tangs spend most of their day picking algae off surfaces.
  • Flow: Moderate to strong — mimics reef conditions

Water Parameters

  • Temperature: 75–82°F
  • pH: 8.1–8.4
  • Salinity: 1.024–1.026 SG
  • Ammonia/Nitrite: 0 ppm
  • Nitrate: below 10 ppm

Diet & Feeding

Primarily herbivorous — algae should make up 80%+ of their diet:

  • Staple: Nori sheets (dried seaweed) clipped to the tank glass or a veggie clip — offer daily
  • Supplements: Marine algae pellets, spirulina flakes, frozen mysis shrimp
  • Important: A diet lacking algae/vegetables leads to Head and Lateral Line Erosion (HLLE) — pitting around the head and lateral line. Ensure plenty of green food.

Aggression

Yellow tangs are peaceful toward most tank mates but highly aggressive toward:

  • Other yellow tangs (unless in very large tanks — 150+ gallons)
  • Similar-shaped fish (other tangs, angelfish) in smaller tanks

They have a sharp scalpel (tang) near the base of their tail fin that they use defensively. Handle with care — the tang can slice skin.

Pricing Note

Yellow tangs were once $30–$40 fish. Following the 2021 ban on aquarium fish collection in Hawaii (their primary source), captive-bred specimens now cost $200–$400+. Captive-bred yellow tangs from Biota and other breeders are available but limited.

Tank Mates

Compatible with most reef fish that are not tang-shaped: clownfish, wrasses, blennies, gobies, cardinalfish, hawkfish. Avoid other tangs in tanks under 125 gallons.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I keep a yellow tang in a 55-gallon tank?

Not recommended. While they will survive, 55 gallons doesn't provide enough swimming space for a fish that grows to 8 inches and is naturally wide-ranging. 75 gallons (4-foot tank) is the minimum for long-term health.

Why is my yellow tang so expensive?

The 2021 Hawaii collection ban drastically reduced supply. Most yellow tangs now available are captive-bred, which is more costly but environmentally responsible.
metaTitle: Yellow Tang Care Guide: Tank Size, Diet & Tank Mates | World of Aquariums
metaDescription: Complete Yellow Tang care guide. Learn about tank size (75+ gal), water parameters, diet, compatible tank mates, and more.

name: Blue Tang (Regal Tang)
diet: Omnivore — nori sheets, marine algae, frozen mysis shrimp, spirulina
description: The blue tang — Dory from Finding Nemo — is one of the most recognizable marine fish with its vivid royal blue body, black palette marking, and yellow tail. While stunning, they grow large (up to 12 inches), are highly susceptible to ich, and need tanks of 75+ gallons. They are not beginner fish despite their popularity following the movies.
careGuide:
Tank Setup

  • Tank size: 75 gallons absolute minimum, 125+ strongly recommended. Blue tangs grow to 12 inches and are extremely active swimmers.
  • Rock work: Extensive rock with swim-throughs and caves. Blue tangs sleep wedged sideways in crevices at night — provide appropriately sized sleeping spots.
  • Flow: Strong — mimics their natural reef habitat

Water Parameters

  • Temperature: 75–82°F
  • pH: 8.1–8.4
  • Salinity: 1.024–1.026 SG
  • Ammonia/Nitrite: 0 ppm
  • Nitrate: below 10 ppm

Ich Susceptibility

Blue tangs are the most ich-prone fish in the marine hobby. Their thin skin and low natural immunity make them a "canary in the coal mine" — if ich is present in your system, the blue tang will show it first. Quarantine all new fish for 30+ days before adding to a tank with a blue tang. Many experienced keepers use UV sterilizers specifically to protect their tangs.

Diet & Feeding

  • Staple: Nori sheets and marine algae — offer daily
  • Supplements: Frozen mysis shrimp, spirulina pellets, marine flakes
  • Important: A primarily algae-based diet prevents HLLE (Head and Lateral Line Erosion)

Sleeping Behavior

Blue tangs sleep on their side, wedged into rock crevices. New owners frequently panic thinking their fish has died. This is completely normal — they will resume normal swimming when lights come on. Their color also fades significantly at night, which further alarms new keepers.

Tank Mates

Generally compatible with other reef fish. They can be kept with yellow tangs and other tang species in 125+ gallon tanks with proper introduction order (add the blue tang last). Aggressive toward their own species — only keep one blue tang per tank unless in a 200+ gallon system.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can a blue tang live in a 55-gallon tank?

No. Blue tangs grow to 12 inches and need extensive swimming space. A juvenile may survive temporarily in a 55, but it's irresponsible long-term. 75 gallons is the absolute minimum; 125+ is appropriate.

Why does my blue tang have white spots?

Almost certainly marine ich (Cryptocaryon irritans). Blue tangs are extremely susceptible. Treat the entire tank with a copper-based medication in a quarantine/hospital tank. Do NOT add copper to a reef tank — it will kill invertebrates.

Why does my blue tang play dead?

They sleep on their side in crevices and fade in color at night. This is normal and not a sign of illness.
metaTitle: Blue Tang (Regal Tang) Care Guide: Tank Size, Diet & Tank Mates | World of Aquariums
metaDescription: Complete Blue Tang (Regal Tang) care guide. Learn about tank size (75+ gal), water parameters, diet, compatible tank mates, and more.

name: Skunk Cleaner Shrimp
diet: Omnivore — fish parasites, frozen mysis shrimp, pellets, scraps
description: Skunk cleaner shrimp are one of the most useful invertebrates in reef tanks. They operate a 'cleaning station' where fish line up to have parasites, dead skin, and debris removed from their bodies and gills. This natural cleaning service helps keep fish healthy and disease-free. They are also strikingly beautiful with their red-and-white striped body and long white antennae.
careGuide:
Cleaning Station Behavior

One of the most fascinating behaviors in the marine world. Cleaner shrimp establish a fixed spot in the tank (usually on a prominent rock) and advertise by waving their long white antennae. Fish voluntarily approach, open their gills, and allow the shrimp to pick off parasites and dead tissue. Even large, predatory fish that would normally eat shrimp will patiently queue up at a cleaning station.

They will also clean your hands if you put them in the tank — a ticklish but charming experience.

Tank Setup

  • Tank size: 20 gallons minimum
  • Rock work: Provide a prominent perch for their cleaning station
  • Acclimation: Drip acclimate for 1–2 hours. Shrimp are extremely sensitive to rapid salinity and temperature changes.

Water Parameters

  • Temperature: 75–82°F
  • pH: 8.1–8.4
  • Salinity: 1.024–1.026 SG
  • Calcium: 350–450 ppm (essential for shell/molt health)
  • Iodine: Supplement if not already present — supports healthy molting

Molting

Cleaner shrimp molt (shed their exoskeleton) every 3–6 weeks. After molting, they are soft and vulnerable for 24–48 hours. Don't remove the discarded shell — other tank inhabitants (including the shrimp itself) will eat it for calcium. If your shrimp is hiding and appears pale or soft, it has likely just molted.

Copper Warning

NEVER use copper-based medications in a tank with cleaner shrimp (or any invertebrate). Copper is lethal to shrimp even in tiny amounts. If treating fish with copper, use a separate hospital/quarantine tank.

Frequently Asked Questions

How many cleaner shrimp can I keep?

2–3 in a typical reef tank (50–100 gallons). They are simultaneous hermaphrodites — any two can pair up and breed. They generally coexist peacefully.

Will cleaner shrimp eat coral?

No. They are completely reef safe. They may occasionally climb on corals, but they don't eat or damage them.

name: White Cloud Mountain Minnow
diet: Omnivore — flakes, micro pellets, frozen daphnia, brine shrimp
description: White cloud mountain minnows are hardy, colorful cold-water fish that are perfect for unheated aquariums and goldfish companions. Their small size, brilliant red fins, and iridescent lateral line make them surprisingly attractive for such a hardy, easy-to-keep species. They are one of the few truly cold-water schooling fish available in the hobby.
careGuide:
Tank Setup

  • Tank size: 10 gallons for a school of 6–8
  • Heater: Not needed — they prefer cooler water (60–72°F) and can tolerate temperatures down to 40°F
  • Plants: Well-planted with both rooted and floating plants

Water Parameters

  • Temperature: 60–72°F (16–22°C) — they tolerate a huge range
  • pH: 6.0–8.0
  • GH: 5–19 dGH

Their tolerance for cold water makes them one of the few fish that can be kept with goldfish (though goldfish may eat them once they grow large enough). They also make excellent unheated tank and outdoor pond fish in temperate climates.

Tank Mates

Best with other cold-water species: goldfish (caution — size difference), other white clouds, rosy barbs, paradise fish, hillstream loaches. They can tolerate tropical temperatures but their lifespan is reduced above 72°F.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can white clouds live outside?

Yes, in temperate climates. They can tolerate temperatures down to about 40°F and make excellent outdoor tub/pond fish during warmer months. Bring them indoors before freezing temperatures.

name: Hillstream Loach
diet: Herbivore — biofilm, algae, blanched vegetables, algae wafers
description: Hillstream loaches are fascinating flat-bodied fish that resemble tiny stingrays. They are specialized for life in fast-flowing, oxygen-rich streams and use their modified pelvic and pectoral fins to grip smooth rocks in strong current. Their unique appearance, algae-eating habits, and cool-water preference make them popular with experienced fishkeepers looking for something different.
careGuide:
Tank Setup — The River Tank

Hillstream loaches require a specific setup that mimics fast-flowing mountain streams:

  • Tank size: 20 gallons minimum
  • Flow: Strong — the most critical requirement. Use a powerhead or wave maker to create strong, directional flow. They need high oxygenation and water movement.
  • Temperature: Cool — 65–75°F. They do not tolerate warm water well.
  • Substrate: Smooth river rocks and pebbles — they cling to and graze on these surfaces
  • Surfaces: Provide lots of smooth, flat rocks for grazing. They eat the biofilm that grows on rock surfaces in high-flow environments.

Water Parameters

  • Temperature: 65–75°F (18–24°C) — warmer water holds less oxygen, which they need
  • pH: 6.5–7.5
  • GH: 4–12 dGH
  • Oxygen: High — strong flow and surface agitation are essential

Diet & Feeding

Primarily biofilm and algae grazers. In a mature tank with strong flow and smooth rocks, biofilm grows continuously on surfaces. Supplement with:

  • Algae wafers (Hikari Algae Wafers, Repashy Soilent Green)
  • Blanched zucchini and cucumber
  • Spirulina wafers

Ensure your tank is mature (3+ months running) before adding hillstream loaches — they need established biofilm.

Frequently Asked Questions

Are hillstream loaches good algae eaters?

Excellent — on par with otocinclus for soft algae and biofilm. They are especially effective on smooth surfaces. They will not eat tough algae like BBA or green spot algae.

Can hillstream loaches live in a normal tropical tank?

Not ideally. They need cooler water (under 75°F) and strong flow. Standard tropical community tanks are too warm and too still for them.

name: Celestial Pearl Danio
diet: Omnivore — micro pellets, crushed flakes, frozen daphnia, baby brine shrimp
description: Celestial pearl danios (also called galaxy rasboras) are tiny, jewel-like fish discovered in Myanmar in 2006. Their dark blue body is covered in iridescent pearl-white spots, with bright orange-red fins striped with black. Despite their exotic appearance, they are hardy and easy to care for — one of the hobby's best-kept secrets for nano tanks.
careGuide:
Tank Setup

  • Tank size: 10 gallons for a group of 8–10
  • Plants: Densely planted — CPDs are shy and need cover to feel secure. They display best behavior in heavily planted tanks with mosses, fine-leaved plants, and floating cover.
  • Lighting: Moderate — subdued lighting brings out their sparkle
  • Flow: Gentle — they come from calm, shallow pools

Water Parameters

  • Temperature: 72–78°F (22–26°C) — cooler than most tropicals
  • pH: 6.5–7.5
  • GH: 2–10 dGH

Behavior

Males are territorial and will display to each other with flared fins — this sparring is harmless and brings out their best colors. Keep more females than males (2:1 ratio) to spread out male attention. In heavily planted tanks, they scatter eggs regularly and may self-sustain a small population if fry have enough cover.

Tank Mates

Only with very small, peaceful species. Their tiny size makes them vulnerable. Ideal companions: cherry shrimp, ember tetras, pygmy corydoras, endlers, and snails. They are a perfect nano tank species.

Frequently Asked Questions

Are CPDs hard to keep?

No — despite their exotic appearance, they are quite hardy once established. The main challenge is their shyness — in sparsely planted tanks or with boisterous tank mates, they hide constantly and lose color.

name: Zebra Danio
diet: Omnivore — flakes, micro pellets, frozen brine shrimp, daphnia
description: Zebra danios are one of the hardiest, most energetic freshwater fish in the hobby. Their horizontal blue and silver stripes and constant darting motion add life to any aquarium. They tolerate a remarkably wide range of conditions, from cool subtropical to warm tropical temperatures, making them ideal for beginners. They are also the species used as the basis for GloFish.
careGuide:
Tank Setup

  • Tank size: 10 gallons for 6. They are extremely active swimmers and need horizontal swimming space despite their small size.
  • Flow: Moderate — they enjoy swimming against current
  • Lid: Important — zebra danios are jumpers when excited or startled

Water Parameters

  • Temperature: 65–77°F — one of the widest temperature ranges of any aquarium fish
  • pH: 6.5–7.5
  • GH: 5–19 dGH

Extreme Hardiness

Zebra danios are often used to cycle new tanks because they tolerate ammonia and nitrite better than almost any other species. While this isn't a recommended practice (fishless cycling is better for the fish), it speaks to their incredible toughness. They tolerate temperature swings, pH fluctuations, and less-than-perfect water quality that would stress most species.

GloFish Connection

Zebra danios are the original GloFish species. The fluorescent genes (from jellyfish and coral) were originally inserted for scientific research — to create fish that glow in the presence of environmental toxins. The ornamental fish hobby adopted them as novelty pets. GloFish danios have identical care requirements to standard zebra danios.

Tank Mates

Good with most community fish. Their speed and energy can intimidate very slow or timid species. Avoid keeping with long-finned, slow fish like bettas — while danios aren't true fin nippers, their constant zooming can stress calm fish.

Frequently Asked Questions

Are zebra danios good for cycling a tank?

They can survive it, but fishless cycling is always preferred. No fish should be subjected to ammonia and nitrite exposure intentionally. If you must fish-in cycle, danios are the most tolerant species, but monitor water parameters closely and do frequent water changes.
